title: Analytical performance evaluation of quantum radar architectures: From single-photon to entangled-noise radars
abstract: This article presents a comprehensive analysis of two classes of quantum radars, including quantum direct-detection and quantum-entangled noise radars. In the first case, inspired by the well-established concept of single-photon LiDARs, we investigated the performance of single-photon radars, in which state-of-the-art single microwave-photon detectors are employed to enhance the detection sensitivity and enable the detection of weaker signals. We derived analytical expressions for the maximum detection range of both classes of quantum radars in terms of the Lambert W function, by considering all relevant system, target, and environmental parameters. Our formulation facilitates direct comparison of noise radars with direct-detection radars and suggests that a quantum-entangled noise radar can be regarded as an enhanced direct-detection radar with an effective threshold signal-to-noise ratio. Furthermore, we applied this framework to classical-correlated noise radars and defined the parameter range enhancement factor (REF) to quantify the superiority of quantum-entangled noise radars over their classical counterparts. Moreover, we introduced a rule-of-thumb for approximating the REF. We also examined the influence of limitations imposed by various microwave detection technologies. Our analysis shows that the conventional antennas limit the potential benefits of quantum-entangled noise radar systems. We also demonstrated that the optimal detection method for these radars is a microwave detector based on a quantum transducer combined with a single optical-photon detector. We showed that, with the current technology, implementing a quantum-entangled noise radar with the maximum detection range on the order of few kilometers is possible. Finally, we explored the potential applications of quantum-entangled noise radars.
summary: This paper provides a detailed theoretical evaluation of quantum radar systems, comparing single-photon direct detection against noise radars utilizing entangled microwave fields. By deriving analytical range equations involving the Lambert W function, the authors quantify the advantage of quantum entanglement through an enhancement factor (REF). The results suggest that these advanced quantum receivers can achieve practical detection ranges using current microwave detector technology.
main_problem: The paper aims to comprehensively analyze and derive analytical expressions for the maximum detection range of various quantum radar architectures, comparing single-photon direct-detection radars against quantum-entangled noise radars.
main_result: Quantum-entangled noise radars show potential for significantly enhanced detection ranges compared to classical systems by leveraging quantum correlations, with implementation feasible at a few kilometers using current technology.
method: The authors derived range equations in terms of the Lambert W function by analyzing detection criteria based on signal-to-noise ratios (SNR) and correlation coefficients ($ho$).
model_or_system: The analysis models three radar types: direct-detection (single-photon), classical noise, and quantum-entangled noise radars utilizing Two-Mode Squeezed Vacuum (TMSV) states. The system relies on microwave detection techniques.
key_observables: Maximum detection range ($R_{\max}$), Parameter Range Enhancement Factor (REF), Pearson correlation coefficient ($ho$), and effective threshold SNR ($	ext{SNR}_{	ext{QI}}^{	ext{th,eff}}$).
important_parameters: Detection threshold ($	ext{SNR}_{	ext{th}}$), transmitted power ($P_t$), scattering cross-section ($\sigma$), and atmospheric absorption coefficients.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the use of advanced detectors (e.g., quantum transducers combined with single optical-photon detectors) and models noise addition to correlated pairs, while noting that conventional antennas limit potential benefits.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate schematic diagrams for direct-detection and noise radar systems, showing how $R_{\max}$ depends on $	ext{SNR}_{	ext{th}}$ and transmitted power for both classical and quantum regimes.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the baseline range equation for direct detection (classical/single-photon), then introducing the correlation mechanism of noise radars, and finally deriving the enhanced range equations specific to quantum-entangled noise radar using correlation metrics like $ho$.
why_interesting: This work bridges quantum optics (squeezed states, entanglement) with applied physics (radar detection), providing quantitative performance benchmarks for next-generation sensing technologies.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 3: The schematic diagram of a mono-static noise radar system. Amp.: Amplifier; ADC: Analog to digital converter; SPDC: Spontaneous parametric down-conversion.

FIG. 4: (Color online) (a) The modified correlation coefficient ˜ρ0, the correlation coefficient ˜ρ0 reported in Ref35, and the correction term ξ versus the amplification gain of the idler mode GI. The noise figure and the effective temperature of the idler amplifier are fixed at NF = 1dB and Teff = 500mK, respectively. (b) The correction term ξ as the function of the idler amplifier temperature Teff and gain GI for the noise figure of NF = 1dB. In both figures, the photon number per mode is set to Ns = 0.1.

FIG. 5: (Color online) The normalized Pearson correlation coefficient ρeff Q /ρQ 0 versus (a) amplification gain of the idler mode GI and gain of the signal mode at the receiver GS,rec for the target at range of R = 1 km, and (b) the integration time τint and the bandwidth of the signal mode Bsig for GI = GS,rec = 80 dB. In both cases, the noise figure and effective temperature of the idler and signal amplifiers are fixed at NF = 1dB and Teff = 100mK, respectively. Moreover, the target range is considered to be R = 1 km, and the photon number per mode is set to Ns = 0.1. Other system parameters are the same as in Fig. 2.
